expanded_description: Distinguishing between physical and chemical properties of matter through observation, measurement, and analysis of how substances behave without changing their identity versus during chemical transformations

core_concept.explanation: Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance's chemical identity, while chemical properties describe how a substance behaves during chemical reactions that transform it into new substances. This distinction is fundamental to identifying materials, predicting behavior, and understanding matter transformations.

image.alt: Comparison diagram showing physical properties (color, density, melting point) vs chemical properties (flammability, reactivity, toxicity)
image.caption: Figure 1.10: Physical properties preserve substance identity; chemical properties involve identity change

sections.introduction: Every substance has characteristic properties that help identify it and predict how it will behave under different conditions. Understanding the difference between physical and chemical properties is essential for proper material handling, identification, and safe laboratory practice.

key_concept.definition: A property of matter is any characteristic that can be observed or measured. Physical properties are observed without changing the substance's identity; chemical properties are observed only when the substance undergoes a chemical change.
key_concept.context: This classification system enables scientists to identify unknown substances, select appropriate materials for specific applications, and predict reaction outcomes.

image.alt: Flowchart for determining whether a property is physical or chemical
image.caption: Figure 1.11: Decision tree for classifying properties of matter

principles.statement: If observing or measuring a property changes the substance into a different substance, it is a chemical property; if the substance remains the same, it is a physical property.
principles.application: When classifying a property, ask: 'After this observation, is the material still the same substance?' If yes → physical; if no → chemical.
principles.analogy: Think of physical properties like describing a person's height or eye color—the person remains the same. Chemical properties are like describing how they react in an argument—their state or behavior fundamentally changes.

sections.section_title: 1.3.2 Physical Properties: Observable Without Identity Change
sections.introduction: Physical properties can be measured or observed without altering the chemical composition of a substance. These properties are essential for identification, quality control, and material selection.

key_concept.definition: A physical property is a characteristic that can be observed or measured without changing the substance's chemical identity or composition.
key_concept.context: Physical properties are further classified as intensive (independent of amount) or extensive (dependent on amount).

image.alt: Collage showing melting ice, measuring density, observing color, testing conductivity
image.caption: Figure 1.12: Common physical properties that don't change substance identity

classifications.value: Use when the property does not depend on the amount of substance present
characteristics: Include density, melting point, boiling point, color, odor, hardness
classifications.behavior: These properties remain constant regardless of how much material you have; ideal for identifying unknown substances

examples.explanation: Gold has a density of $19.3\ \text{g/cm}^3$ whether you have a small nugget or a large bar. This intensive property helps distinguish gold from fool's gold (pyrite, $5.0\ \text{g/cm}^3$).

image.alt: Gold bar and pyrite sample with density measurements
image.caption: Density is an intensive property useful for material identification

examples.explanation: Water melts at $0^\circ\text{C}$ ($32^\circ\text{F}$) regardless of whether you have an ice cube or an iceberg. This property helps identify pure water versus salt water (lower melting point).
examples.calculation: Melting point determination: Heat sample slowly, record temperature when solid begins to melt

examples.explanation: Ethanol boils at $78.4^\circ\text{C}$ at standard pressure. This intensive property distinguishes it from water ($100^\circ\text{C}$) and methanol ($64.7^\circ\text{C}$).

classifications.value: Use when the property depends on the amount of substance present
characteristics: Include mass, volume, length, total energy
classifications.behavior: These properties change when you change the amount of material; must be combined with intensive properties for identification

examples.explanation: A $10\ \text{g}$ sample of copper has different mass than a $100\ \text{g}$ sample, but both have the same density ($8.96\ \text{g/cm}^3$). Mass alone cannot identify the substance.
Volume depends on container size and amount poured. $50\ \text{mL}$ of water has the same density as $500\ \text{mL}$ of water, but different volume.

classifications.note: Extensive properties can be converted to intensive properties by taking ratios (e.g., density = mass/volume)

principles.statement: Physical properties can be quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive), and both types are valuable for substance identification.
principles.application: Quantitative: density = $1.00\ \text{g/mL}$; Qualitative: colorless, odorless liquid. Use both for complete characterization.
principles.example: Water identification: Quantitative (density $1.00\ \text{g/mL}$, boiling point $100^\circ\text{C}$) + Qualitative (clear, colorless, odorless liquid)

sections.section_title: 1.3.3 Chemical Properties: Observed During Identity Change
sections.introduction: Chemical properties describe how a substance behaves when it interacts with other substances or energy in ways that change its chemical identity. These properties can only be observed through chemical reactions.

key_concept.definition: A chemical property is a characteristic that describes a substance's ability to undergo chemical changes and form new substances with different properties.
key_concept.context: Chemical properties are revealed only when the substance participates in chemical reactions; they cannot be observed without changing the substance's identity.

image.alt: Images showing rust formation, combustion, acid-base reactions, tarnishing
image.caption: Figure 1.13: Chemical properties are observed through reactions that create new substances

classifications.value: Use when describing how a substance interacts with other chemicals
classifications.behavior: These properties determine how safely a substance can be stored and handled; highly reactive materials require special precautions

examples.explanation: Gasoline readily combusts in air: $2\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18(l)} + 25\text{O}_{2(g)} \rightarrow 16\text{CO}_{2(g)} + 18\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)} + \text{energy}$. This chemical property requires storage away from ignition sources.

image.alt: Flammable liquid warning symbol and combustion demonstration
image.caption: Flammability is a chemical property—combustion creates new substances

examples.explanation: Sodium metal reacts violently with water: $2\text{Na}_{(s)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH}_{(aq)} + \text{H}_{2(g)} + \text{heat}$. The sodium is transformed into sodium hydroxide—a different substance.
examples.calculation: Reaction is exothermic; $\Delta H < 0$; hydrogen gas produced is flammable

examples.explanation: Iron reacts with oxygen and water: $4\text{Fe}_{(s)} + 3\text{O}_{2(g)} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_{3(s)}$. The reddish-brown rust ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$) is chemically different from metallic iron.
examples.note: Rusting is a slow chemical property; prevents further corrosion if layer is intact (like aluminum oxide)

classifications.value: Use when describing a substance's resistance to chemical change
classifications.behavior: Stable substances resist change under normal conditions; unstable substances may decompose spontaneously or require special storage

examples.explanation: Calcium carbonate decomposes at high temperature: $\text{CaCO}_{3(s)} \xrightarrow{840^\circ\text{C}} \text{CaO}_{(s)} + \text{CO}_{2(g)}$. Below $840^\circ\text{C}$, it remains stable—this temperature is a chemical property.
examples.calculation: Decomposition temperature indicates thermal stability limit

examples.explanation: Silver bromide decomposes in light: $2\text{AgBr}_{(s)} \xrightarrow{\text{light}} 2\text{Ag}_{(s)} + \text{Br}_{2(g)}$. This photochemical property is used in photography; requires dark storage.

image.alt: Silver bromide before and after light exposure showing darkening
image.caption: Photochemical decomposition is a chemical property of silver halides

classifications.value: Use when describing how a substance interacts with living systems
classifications.behavior: These properties determine health hazards and environmental impact; require careful handling and disposal

examples.explanation: Lead ions ($\text{Pb}^{2+}$) interfere with enzyme function and neurological development. This chemical property requires special handling, PPE, and hazardous waste disposal.
examples.note: Toxicity is a chemical property because it involves chemical interactions with biological molecules

examples.explanation: Some plastics (e.g., PLA) undergo enzymatic breakdown: $(\text{C}_3\text{H}_4\text{O}_2)_n + n\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{enzymes}} n\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3$. Others (e.g., polyethylene) resist biodegradation—this is a chemical property.
examples.calculation: Biodegradation rate measured by $\text{CO}_2$ evolution or mass loss over time

key_concept.definition: The key distinction: physical properties can be observed without changing what the substance IS; chemical properties describe what the substance CAN DO when it changes into something else.
key_concept.context: This distinction is critical for safety assessments, material selection, and predicting behavior in different environments.

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison table of physical vs chemical properties with examples
image.caption: Figure 1.14: Quick reference for distinguishing property types

process_flow.description: Determine how the property is being observed or measured.
process_flow.mechanism: Physical: measurement, observation, physical manipulation; Chemical: reaction with other substances, energy input causing transformation
process_flow.result: Initial classification based on observation type
process_flow.organization: Ask: 'Does this require a chemical reaction to observe?'

process_flow.description: Determine if the substance's chemical identity changes during observation.
process_flow.mechanism: Physical: same molecules/atoms before and after; Chemical: new substances formed with different formulas
process_flow.result: No identity change → physical property; Identity change → chemical property

process_flow.description: Consider whether the change can be reversed by physical means.
process_flow.mechanism: Physical changes are often reversible (melting/freezing); chemical changes usually require another chemical reaction to reverse
process_flow.result: Easily reversible → likely physical; Requires chemical reaction to reverse → chemical
process_flow.note: Some physical changes are difficult to reverse (breaking glass) but still don't change chemical identity

process_flow.description: Compare chemical formulas before and after the observation.
process_flow.mechanism: Physical: same formula (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(s)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$); Chemical: different formula (e.g., $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$)
process_flow.result: Same formula → physical property; Different formula → chemical property
process_flow.organization: Write balanced equations to verify substance identity

worked_examples.title: Classifying Properties of Copper
worked_examples.problem: Classify each property of copper as physical or chemical: (a) reddish-brown color, (b) reacts with nitric acid to produce blue solution, (c) density of $8.96\ \text{g/cm}^3$, (d) tarnishes in moist air forming green patina.

step_by_step.explanation: Color can be observed without changing copper into a different substance. The copper remains $\text{Cu}$.
Reaction produces new substances: $3\text{Cu}_{(s)} + 8\text{HNO}_{3(aq)} \rightarrow 3\text{Cu(NO}_3)_{2(aq)} + 2\text{NO}_{(g)} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$. Copper is transformed.
Density is mass/volume ratio. Measuring doesn't change copper's identity. Intensive physical property.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Physical, (b) Chemical, (c) Physical, (d) Chemical. Properties (a) and (c) can be observed without changing copper's identity; (b) and (d) involve chemical reactions that transform copper into different substances.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using identity change test to classify properties

image.alt: Copper samples showing color, density measurement, acid reaction, and patina formation
image.caption: Copper demonstrating both physical and chemical properties

worked_examples.title: Property Classification Challenge
worked_examples.problem: A student observes: (1) a liquid boils at $78^\circ\text{C}$, (2) the vapor burns with a blue flame, (3) the liquid dissolves sugar, (4) the liquid reacts with sodium metal. Classify each observation.

step_by_step.explanation: Boiling is a phase change: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}_{(l)} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}_{(g)}$. Same molecular formula before and after.
Burning produces $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$. New substances formed.
Sugar molecules disperse in liquid but remain $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}$. Can recover sugar by evaporation.
Produces hydrogen gas and alkoxide: $2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\text{Na} \rightarrow 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{ONa} + \text{H}_2$. New substances formed.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Physical property (boiling point), (2) Chemical property (flammability), (3) Physical property (solvency), (4) Chemical property (reactivity with metals). The liquid is likely ethanol based on boiling point and properties.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying identity preservation test to multiple observations

sections.introduction: Understanding physical and chemical properties has real-world applications in material selection, safety protocols, quality control, and environmental protection.

key_concept.definition: Property classification guides decision-making in chemistry, engineering, medicine, and environmental science by predicting how materials will behave under different conditions.
key_concept.context: Safety Data Sheets (SDS) list both physical and chemical properties to inform safe handling, storage, and emergency response.

classifications.value: Use property data to select appropriate materials for specific applications
classifications.behavior: Engineers match material properties to application requirements; wrong selection leads to failure

examples.explanation: Aluminum alloys chosen for low density ($2.7\ \text{g/cm}^3$) and high strength-to-weight ratio. Titanium used for high-temperature engine parts (melting point $1668^\circ\text{C}$). These physical properties optimize performance.

image.alt: Aircraft components showing different metal alloys used
image.caption: Physical properties guide material selection in aerospace engineering

examples.explanation: Steel chosen for tensile strength; concrete for compressive strength. Chemical property: corrosion resistance through galvanization or protective coatings prevents structural failure.
examples.calculation: Corrosion rate measured in $\text{mm/year}$; protective coatings extend service life

classifications.value: Use chemical properties to identify and mitigate hazards
classifications.behavior: Chemical properties determine storage requirements, PPE needs, and emergency response procedures

examples.explanation: Ethanol (flash point $13^\circ\text{C}$) stored in flammable cabinet away from oxidizers. Chemical property (flammability) dictates storage protocol to prevent fires.
examples.note: Flash point is a physical property; flammability is a chemical property—both inform safety

examples.explanation: Strong acids and bases stored separately due to chemical property: violent neutralization reaction $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{heat}$. Mixing releases significant heat and may cause splashing.
examples.calculation: Neutralization is exothermic; $\Delta H = -57.3\ \text{kJ/mol}$ for strong acid-base

classifications.value: Use physical properties to verify material purity and identity
classifications.behavior: Deviations from expected physical properties indicate impurities or misidentification

examples.explanation: Aspirin purity verified by melting point ($135^\circ\text{C}$ for pure). Lower or broad melting range indicates impurities. Physical property ensures drug safety and efficacy.

image.alt: Melting point apparatus with capillary tube sample
image.caption: Melting point determination for pharmaceutical quality control

examples.explanation: Diamond identified by hardness (10 on Mohs scale), density ($3.51\ \text{g/cm}^3$), and refractive index (2.42). These physical properties distinguish from cubic zirconia and other simulants.
examples.calculation: Refractive index measured using refractometer; $n_D = 2.417$ for diamond

summary: Physical properties can be observed or measured without changing a substance's chemical identity (e.g., color, density, melting point), while chemical properties describe how a substance behaves during reactions that transform it into new substances (e.g., flammability, reactivity, toxicity). Intensive physical properties (independent of amount) are most useful for identification; extensive properties (dependent on amount) must be combined with intensive properties. The key test: if observing the property changes what the substance IS, it's chemical; if the substance remains the same, it's physical. This distinction guides material selection, safety protocols, quality control, and environmental protection. Always consider both physical and chemical properties when handling, storing, or using materials in laboratory and industrial settings.
